next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 5

The departure board at Caldera kept time in red digits big enough to shame everybody. CAR 8 RELEASE in 00:18. SOUTHBOUND SLOT CONFIRMED. Under the board the bay floor sweated old coolant, mop water, and a sweetness from the vending-machine syrup line that had been leaking for weeks. Marisol stood at the wall terminal with her supervisor passcard clipped to her pocket and bleach drying white in the creases of her knuckles. The clock lost a minute while she watched it. That was how the line did most of its violence. Not with a dramatic sound. With numbers continuing.

She had spent twenty years learning which messes were considered real and which were only labor. Blood on restraint vinyl was labor. Urine pooled under a toilet flange was labor. Vomit in a family bunk was labor unless it got into the foam, and even then there were grades, there were codes, there were ways to write a body back into dispatch. But once waste was described as airborne, once contamination crossed from surface to system, no one in compliance wanted to own the release. The company could forgive filth. It could not forgive the words that made filth legible.

Car 8 was amber, not red. Aft-lavatory ammonia high. Moisture under the lower bunks. The sensor history showed one brief backspit after intake, then a stable line. It was a tired car and a hot day. Nothing unusual. Marisol touched the incident field. The rubber of the glove caught on the screen.

`Cross-surface fecal aerosol following blackwater backspit during hold. Soft material exposure unresolved. Full deep clean required before di...
open_hooks_to_carry: Explain ancestral admissibility only through lived paperwork pressure, not abstract exposition.
Make Teresa persuasive in institutional terms rather than cartoonishly cruel.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...rcode facing out. A lazy check would see plastic and label and move along. A careful check would find the loss. Let careful arrive later.

She tucked the recorder inside the waistband of her work pants, under her shirt, where the hard rectangle rested against the soft lower belly she still thought of as the part of her body Gabriel had once occupied without asking permission. The thought came and she nearly flinched from it. She shut the property-room light off, keyed the door behind her, and walked back into the corridor at the pace of a woman who had completed a boring errand.

On the platform side window, dusk had gone from yellow to a flat iron blue. A commuter train pulled out, taking its clean passengers home. Farther down the yard she could see Ivo near the edge of the maintenance lane, one shoulder against a post, phone to his ear at last. He was listening more than speaking, head bent, the posture Marisol had seen once before when Gabriel was ten and had split his chin on a sink and Ivo had stood outside the clinic calling his daughter to explain why he'd miss dinner. In the trauma-room corridor on the other side, Paloma was adjusting her basil cups on a sill under a light, turning the leaves toward whatever weak brightness the building gave.

The recorder pressed against Marisol with each step, small and undeniable. The hotline had given her a ticket number and nothing else. The machine at her waist had weight. It could be carried. It could be hidden. It had survived somebody else's hands. By the time she reached the dorm stairs, it had already warmed to her body.
state_deltas_required: Ancestral-admissibility policy, Teresa's scrutiny, and the train's imminent southbound departure.
Marisol learns Gabriel's case is structurally locked and realizes many other detainees are being assigned to false countries through the same mechanism.
